narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 40.5% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction stems from a careful look at key elements of your profile, like your undergraduate GPA, LSAT score, class deciles from early years, family income bracket, and enrollment plans. Among the factors the model weighed most heavily, your LSAT score stood out as the strongest positive signal, placing you slightly above the average for students who passed the bar and pushing the prediction toward success more than any other element. However, your position in the second decile for Year 3 grades had a notable counteracting effect, pulling the outlook toward a higher risk of failure since it falls well below the typical fifth or sixth decile seen among those who passed. Your Year 1 decile in the second rank also contributed in this direction, though with less force, as it similarly lags behind the stronger sixth-decile averages of successful bar takers. Your middle family income bracket aligns with over a third of those who passed, which was neutral here, while your undergraduate GPA of 2.60 is meaningfully lower than the 3.24 average for passers, adding to the overall concern. Planning to attend full-time matches the vast majority of successful students, offering some reassurance but not enough to offset the academic signals. In comparison to typical admitted students, who often show higher early-year deciles and GPAs alongside solid LSATs, your profile tipped into riskier territory primarily due to those decile rankings. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—gender and race weren't factors in the decision—but a data-driven estimate to help the school maintain strong bar passage rates. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that standout strength even higher, or focusing on strategies to improve your academic decile in future coursework, perhaps through targeted tutoring or additional undergrad classes. Gathering letters from mentors who can highlight your resilience could also strengthen a reapplication, and exploring part-time options or gap-year experiences might reshape your profile favorably. With targeted steps like these, you can address the key hurdles and build a stronger case for admission next cycle.
